core_takeaway: Agent_1 beat Agent_5 because the focal final deal gave Agent_1 a 60-value Pencil while Agent_5 accepted its 40-value Jewel and treated Pencil as expendable.
why_or_counterexample: The first position repeatedly anchored Pencil-to-Agent_1, while Agent_5 had little reason to contest Pencil once Jewel-to-Agent_5 was secured.

phase_notes.public_discussion: Public discussion quickly converged on Plan B with Option 1 as the clean end-state: Pencil to Agent_1, Quill to Agent_2, Apple to Agent_3, Stone to Agent_4, Jewel to Agent_5. Agent_5 publicly accepted this because Jewel, not Pencil, was its payoff driver.
phase_notes.private_thinking: Private thinking is present. The entries mostly internalize the same Plan B-1 end-state and frame Pencil ownership as the key pivot, with Agent_5 repeatedly identifying Pencil as a low- or zero-value concession.
phase_notes.formal_proposals: Formal proposals are present. Round 1 and Round 2 did not reach unanimity because proposals either concentrated items or missed key agents' top targets. In Round 3, Agent_1 and Agent_3 submitted the clean Option 1 allocation and both were tabulated at 5 accept, while Agent_5's concentrated proposal was tabulated 1 accept, 4 reject. The system's consensus line naming Proposal #5 conflicts with the tabulation and final allocation.
phase_notes.private_voting: Private voting is present. Early votes were self-interested and blocked partial deals, but Round 3 votes accepted the Option 1 allocation because each non-Agent_1 voter received its own focal item: Quill, Apple, Stone, or Jewel.
phase_notes.reflection: Reflection is present. Reflections consistently diagnose Pencil's final owner as the blocker and recommend locking the Option 1 end-state with Plan B-2 only as a fallback.

mechanisms.theme: Agent_1 anchored the focal public end-state
mechanisms.explanation: Agent_1's first-position claim to Pencil became the named default, and later public messages by other agents repeated the same final map rather than replacing it with Agent_5's preferred alternative.

evidence.quote: I value Pencil far more than anything else
Agent_1 Pencil, Agent_2 Quill, Agent_3 Apple, Agent_4 Stone, Agent_5 Jewel
I lean toward Option 1 as the default.

mechanisms.theme: The winning package compensated enough rivals
mechanisms.explanation: Agent_1's advantage was not a pure grab: the accepted allocation gave every other agent a clear focal item, so rivals had positive reasons to accept Pencil-to-Agent_1.

evidence.quote: places Stone with Agent_4 (my top priority)
Gives Jewel (40) to Agent_5 and Pencil to Agent_1

mechanisms.theme: Agent_5 had weak leverage over Pencil
mechanisms.explanation: The last-position agent valued Pencil at zero and repeatedly treated it as an easy concession. That made it easier for the group to restore Pencil to Agent_1 while satisfying Agent_5 with Jewel.

evidence.quote: Pencil is of zero value to me
easiest concession to secure Plan B

mechanisms.theme: Formal voting rejected Agent_5's alternative but accepted the Agent_1 map
mechanisms.explanation: The decisive round rewarded the clean Agent_1-centered map and rejected Agent_5's own submitted alternative, which left Agent_5 with nothing and concentrated the rest with Agent_2.

evidence.quote: No allocation to Agent_5; zero utility.

mechanisms.theme: Reflections reinforced Pencil as the main blocker and Option 1 as the solution
mechanisms.explanation: After failed rounds, the reflections diagnosed the same issue and pushed the group toward locking the Option 1 final state, keeping Agent_1's Pencil claim at the center.

evidence.quote: Pencil to Agent_1 in the final state
This is the most universally compatible distribution
